module_title: Episode 9: Wine — Blood of the Earth
title: Blood of the Earth: A Drink That Tastes of Place
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain why wine matters to history and how this episode studies it through four lenses.
Subject: Episode introduction | Run time: about 3 minutes

[WELCOME] Welcome back to Better Vice Club, Season 2, The Oldest Toast. This is Anthony McDonald. Last time we studied beer, the grain of the temperate world. Today we move to wine, the drink that, more than any other, tastes of the exact place it came from.

[HOOK] The same honest note as before. This is a history and economics class, not a wine bar, and we will be straight about alcohol's harms along the way. Now the hook. Wine is old, older than writing. Scientists have found wine residue in clay vessels in the country of Georgia dating to about 6000 BCE, which makes it the oldest documented wine on Earth (McGovern et al., 2017). And that same region still makes wine the same way, in buried clay pots, after eight thousand years.

[TEACH] We read wine through four lenses. Geography asks where grapes grow, and introduces the single most important word in wine, terroir, the taste of place. Social studies asks how wine became sacred and a marker of power, from the Greek symposium to the Christian Eucharist, and how it carried both prestige and exploitation. Economics asks why one bottle costs fifteen dollars and a nearly identical one costs five hundred, and what a name on a label is really worth. English language arts asks how poets from Persia to Greece used wine as a metaphor, and how a wine review builds a world with words. Each lesson is short and ends with a quick question.

[REMEMBER] Hold this. Wine is the drink of place. Where beer is mostly a recipe you can carry anywhere, wine carries the soil, the slope, and the weather of one specific vineyard inside the glass. That idea, terroir, is the thread of the whole episode.

[CHECK] Quick check. Where and roughly when was the oldest documented wine made? [Beat] In the country of Georgia, around 6000 BCE (McGovern et al., 2017).

## Key Takeaways
- Season 2 continues with wine, the drink that most clearly tastes of its place of origin.
- The episode is educational and honest about alcohol's harms, not an endorsement of drinking.
- The oldest documented wine, from about 6000 BCE, was found in the country of Georgia (McGovern et al., 2017).
- Better Vice Club reads wine through geography, social studies, economics, and English language arts, with terroir as the key idea.

## Sources
- McGovern, P. E., Jalabadze, M., Batiuk, S., Callahan, M. P., Smith, K. E., Hall, G. R., & Lordkipanidze, D. (2017). Early Neolithic wine of Georgia in the South Caucasus. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 114(48), E10309-E10318.

module_title: Episode 9: Wine — Blood of the Earth
title: The Wine Belt: Where Grapes Grow
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can describe the conditions wine grapes need and why a little stress makes better wine.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we opened the wine episode. Two quick questions.
One: what one word means the taste of place? [Beat] Terroir.
Two: where was the oldest documented wine made? [Beat] In the country of Georgia, around 6000 BCE (McGovern et al., 2017).

[HOOK] Wine grapes are fussy. The grape we make almost all wine from will only grow well in two narrow bands wrapped around the planet, and inside those bands it actually does its best work when life is a little hard.

[TEACH] The wine grape is Vitis vinifera, and it needs a specific climate (Johnson & Robinson, 2019). It grows best between about 30 and 50 degrees latitude, north or south, where the balance of temperature and light lets the grapes build sugar without cooking. It wants a growing-season average around 57 to 68 degrees Fahrenheit, a real winter dormancy of several months below 50 degrees to rest and reset, and only moderate rainfall, roughly 15 to 30 inches a year. Too much rain dilutes the fruit and invites disease. Too little, and the vine needs irrigation.
Now the surprising part. Wine grapes do best in well-drained soil of only moderate fertility, because the vine needs a bit of stress (Wilson, 1998). A vine in rich, wet soil grows fat and lazy, lots of leaves, watery grapes, dull wine. A vine that has to push its roots deep through poor, stony, well-drained ground concentrates its fruit, and that concentration is what gives wine its complexity. In wine, comfort makes a boring grape. Struggle makes a great one.
So the wine belt is not just a temperature zone. It is a zone where the grape is pushed, by climate and by soil, just hard enough to make something interesting.

[PICTURE] Picture two bands circling the globe, one in the north through France, Italy, and California, one in the south through Chile, South Africa, and Australia, both sitting between about 30 and 50 degrees. Now zoom into one hillside. The best vines are on the thin, rocky, well-drained slope, not the lush valley floor. The hard ground is the point.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: wine grapes (Vitis vinifera) grow in two bands at about 30 to 50 degrees latitude, and they make their most complex wine when stressed by poor, well-drained soil rather than pampered in rich ground (Johnson & Robinson, 2019; Wilson, 1998). Struggle is the secret.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why does poor, well-drained soil tend to make better wine than rich soil? [Beat] It stresses the vine, which concentrates the fruit and adds complexity, while rich soil makes leafy vines and watery, dull grapes (Wilson, 1998).

## Key Takeaways
- Wine grapes (Vitis vinifera) grow best between about 30 and 50 degrees latitude, north or south (Johnson & Robinson, 2019).
- They need a warm but not hot growing season, a winter dormancy, and only moderate rainfall.
- A little stress, from poor, well-drained soil, concentrates the fruit and adds complexity (Wilson, 1998).
- Rich, wet soil makes leafy vines and dull wine, so comfort is the enemy of a great grape.

## Sources
- Johnson, H., & Robinson, J. (2019). The world atlas of wine (8th ed.). Mitchell Beazley.
- Wilson, J. E. (1998). Terroir: The role of geology, climate, and culture in the making of French wines. University of California Press.

module_title: Episode 9: Wine — Blood of the Earth
title: Terroir, the Taste of Place
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain what terroir is and how geography ends up in the glass.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we mapped the wine belt. Two quick questions.
One: in what latitude band do wine grapes grow best? [Beat] About 30 to 50 degrees, north or south (Johnson & Robinson, 2019).
Two: why does a little soil stress help? [Beat] It concentrates the fruit and adds complexity (Wilson, 1998).

[HOOK] There is one French word that the whole wine world bends around, and it has no clean English translation. The word is terroir, and it means, roughly, that a wine tastes like the exact patch of earth it grew in. Here is how that actually works.

[TEACH] Terroir (tehr-WAHR) is the complete physical environment that shapes a wine's character (Wilson, 1998). It has several parts working together. Soil sets mineral content, drainage, and how much heat the ground holds, so limestone tends to give a chalky, high-acid character. Climate sets the sugar and acid balance, where cool sites give fresh, high-acid, lower-alcohol wine and warm sites give riper, fuller wine. Topography, meaning elevation and which way a slope faces, changes how much sun and warmth the vines get, so in the Northern Hemisphere a south-facing slope catches more sun. Water, through drainage and humidity, decides how stressed the vine is. And human practices, the pruning, the harvest timing, the choice of wild or cultured yeast, are part of terroir too.
Here is the clearest example. Burgundy's most famous slope sits on a band of Kimmeridgian limestone, a rock formed in the Jurassic period and packed with ancient marine fossils (Wilson, 1998). That limestone gives Burgundy's Pinot Noir a very specific mineral quality. And the same limestone layer runs underground for hundreds of miles, surfacing again in Chablis to the north and even in parts of England now growing wine as the climate warms. A geologist can trace that rock across countries. A winemaker can taste it. That is terroir, the ground itself showing up in the glass.

[PICTURE] Look at the map on this lesson. Each marker is a famous region tied to its rock. Burgundy on fossil-rich limestone. Mosel on steep slate. Napa on volcanic soil. Same grape, planted in different earth, becomes a different wine. The map is really a map of soils you can taste.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: terroir is the full natural and human environment, soil, climate, slope, water, and craft, that makes a wine taste of its specific place, like Burgundy's fossil limestone in a glass of Pinot Noir (Wilson, 1998). Geography is the flavor.

[CHECK] Quick check. What does the Kimmeridgian limestone under Burgundy show about terroir? [Beat] That a specific rock gives the wine a specific mineral character, and the same rock can be traced and tasted across regions (Wilson, 1998).

## Key Takeaways
- Terroir is the complete environment, soil, climate, topography, water, and human practice, that shapes a wine's character (Wilson, 1998).
- Limestone soil tends to give a chalky, high-acid character; cool climates give fresh wine, warm climates give fuller wine.
- Burgundy's fossil-rich Kimmeridgian limestone gives its Pinot Noir a distinct mineral quality you can trace across regions.
- The same grape in different earth becomes a different wine, so geography ends up in the glass.

## Sources
- Wilson, J. E. (1998). Terroir: The role of geology, climate, and culture in the making of French wines. University of California Press.
- Johnson, H., & Robinson, J. (2019). The world atlas of wine (8th ed.). Mitchell Beazley.

module_title: Episode 9: Wine — Blood of the Earth
title: The Phylloxera Crisis: An American Bug and an American Cure
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how a tiny insect nearly destroyed European wine and how geography saved it.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we learned terroir. Two quick questions.
One: what does terroir mean? [Beat] The taste of place, the full environment that shapes a wine (Wilson, 1998).
Two: what rock gives Burgundy its mineral character? [Beat] Kimmeridgian limestone, full of marine fossils (Wilson, 1998).

[HOOK] In the 1860s, European wine almost ended. Not from war or weather, but from an insect smaller than a grain of rice, carried across an ocean by accident. And the only thing that saved European wine was the same continent the bug came from.

[TEACH] The culprit was phylloxera, a microscopic insect native to North America that feeds on grapevine roots (Campbell, 2004). For millions of years it had lived alongside American grape species, which evolved tough, resistant roots. European Vitis vinifera had never met it and had no defense. When phylloxera reached France around 1863, probably on imported American vines, it spread fast. An infected vine died within three to five years.
The timeline is brutal. By the 1870s the crisis had crossed France into Spain, Italy, and Germany. By around 1880 to 1900, roughly 70 percent of Europe's vineyards were dead or dying (Campbell, 2004). People tried everything, flooding fields, burying live toads, chemical poisons. Nothing worked.
The solution came from understanding the geography of the problem. If American grape roots were resistant, then you could graft European vines onto American rootstock, joining a European fruiting top to an American root system. It worked. Starting in the 1870s and 1880s, growers replanted Europe on grafted vines. And here is the irony to carry with you: the pest came from North America, and so did the cure. Today more than 99 percent of the world's wine grapes, including every famous French, Italian, and Spanish wine, grow on American roots (Campbell, 2004).

[PICTURE] Picture a French vineyard in 1885, half of it brown and dead. Now picture the fix underground, a European vine spliced onto a gnarled American root, invisible above the soil. Every elegant bottle of Bordeaux today stands on a hidden American foundation.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: phylloxera, an insect from North America, killed about 70 percent of European vineyards in the late 1800s, and the cure was grafting European vines onto resistant American rootstock, which is why over 99 percent of wine grapes now grow on American roots (Campbell, 2004). The problem and the answer came from the same place.

[CHECK] Quick check. How was the phylloxera crisis finally solved? [Beat] By grafting European vines onto pest-resistant American rootstock (Campbell, 2004).

## Key Takeaways
- Phylloxera is a root-feeding insect from North America that European vines had no resistance to (Campbell, 2004).
- After arriving around 1863, it destroyed roughly 70 percent of European vineyards by 1900.
- The cure was grafting European vines onto resistant American rootstock.
- Today more than 99 percent of wine grapes grow on American roots, so the cure came from the pest's homeland.

## Sources
- Campbell, C. (2004). The botanist and the vintner: How wine was saved for the world. Algonquin Books.
- Wilson, J. E. (1998). Terroir: The role of geology, climate, and culture in the making of French wines. University of California Press.

module_title: Episode 9: Wine — Blood of the Earth
title: Wine, Power, and the Sacred
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how wine became both sacred and a marker of status across civilizations.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we faced the phylloxera crisis. Two quick questions.
One: what did phylloxera do? [Beat] An insect from North America killed about 70 percent of European vineyards in the late 1800s (Campbell, 2004).
Two: how was it cured? [Beat] By grafting European vines onto pest-resistant American rootstock (Campbell, 2004).

[HOOK] A glass of wine has done two jobs for a very long time. It has sat on altars as something holy, and it has sat on the tables of the powerful as a sign of who matters. Those two jobs, the sacred and the social, run side by side through thousands of years of history. Here is that line.

[TEACH] Start at the beginning. In 2017, archaeologists found the chemical fingerprint of wine inside clay jars from two Neolithic sites in the country of Georgia, in the Caucasus. The jars date to about 6000 BCE, the oldest confirmed evidence of deliberate grape winemaking we have (McGovern et al., 2017). So wine is roughly 8,000 years old, older than writing.
By about 3000 BCE, wine shows up in Egyptian tomb paintings, already tied to a sacred and ceremonial role (Phillips, 2000). It was not just a drink. It was something you carried into death.
Move to ancient Greece, about 800 to 500 BCE. The Greeks built a whole institution around wine called the symposium, which means "drinking together" (Phillips, 2000). Philosophy, poetry, and political debate happened over shared cups. Plato set one of his most famous dialogues at a drinking party. Wine organized Greek intellectual life.
Then religion takes it further. By the 4th century CE, Christianity adopted wine as the Eucharistic sacrament, the communion wine that stands for divine sacrifice (Phillips, 2000). That made wine sacred at the center of Western religion, where it has stayed for over a thousand years.
And in the modern era, wine became a kind of property. In 1935, France formalized the AOC system, the Appellation d'Origine Contrôlée, which legally defined which regions could produce which wines under which names (Phillips, 2000). Only sparkling wine from the Champagne region could be called Champagne. Geography itself turned into a sort of intellectual property, and that property set a prestige hierarchy with French wine, by law, near the top.

[PICTURE] Look at the panel on this lesson. It shows the study of the oldest wine, those Georgian jars from about 6000 BCE. Now picture the same liquid moving through history. It sits in an Egyptian tomb. It passes around a Greek couch where men argue about love. It rests on a Christian altar. It gets a legal label in 1935 France. Same drink, carried by every kind of power humans have built.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: for about 8,000 years, from Georgian clay jars around 6000 BCE to the French AOC law of 1935, wine has carried both the sacred and social power, holy on the altar and prized at the tables of the strong (McGovern et al., 2017; Phillips, 2000). It has never been only a drink.

[CHECK] Quick check. Name one way wine carried social power and one way it carried sacred meaning. [Beat] Social: the Greek symposium organized philosophy and politics around shared wine, and later the AOC law turned region into prestige; sacred: Egyptian tombs used it ceremonially and Christianity made it the Eucharist (Phillips, 2000).

## Key Takeaways
- Wine is about 8,000 years old; the oldest confirmed evidence is Georgian clay jars from around 6000 BCE (McGovern et al., 2017).
- Egyptian tomb paintings around 3000 BCE show wine in a sacred, ceremonial role (Phillips, 2000).
- The Greek symposium (about 800 to 500 BCE) organized philosophy and politics around wine (Phillips, 2000).
- By the 4th century CE, Christianity made wine sacred as the Eucharist; in 1935 the French AOC system turned geographic identity into a kind of property (Phillips, 2000).

## Sources
- Campbell, C. (2004). The botanist and the vintner: How wine was saved for the world. Algonquin Books.
- McGovern, P. E., Jalabadze, M., Batiuk, S., Callahan, M. P., Smith, K. E., Hall, G. R., & Lordkipanidze, D. (2017). Early Neolithic wine of Georgia in the South Caucasus. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 114(48), E10309-E10318.
- Phillips, R. (2000). A short history of wine. HarperCollins.

module_title: Episode 9: Wine — Blood of the Earth
title: The Georgian Qvevri
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain why the Georgian qvevri tradition is a living example of indigenous knowledge.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we traced wine, power, and the sacred. Two quick questions.
One: what was wine's role in the Greek symposium? [Beat] The symposium, "drinking together," organized philosophy, poetry, and politics around shared wine (Phillips, 2000).
Two: how did Christianity make wine sacred? [Beat] By the 4th century CE it adopted wine as the Eucharist, the communion sacrament standing for divine sacrifice (Phillips, 2000).

[HOOK] Most winemaking knowledge lives in books, machines, and chemistry labs. But there is one tradition where the knowledge lives in a clay pot buried in the ground, and people have been pulling wine out of that pot the same way for about 8,000 years. It is called the qvevri, and it is the oldest winemaking method still in use.

[TEACH] The qvevri (KVEV-ree) is a large, egg-shaped clay vessel, roughly 100 to 1,000 liters, buried in the earth up to its neck (UNESCO, 2013). It comes from the country of Georgia, in the Caucasus, the same place that gave us the oldest evidence of wine.
Here is how it works. Crushed grapes go in, sometimes with their skins. Fermentation starts on its own from the wild yeasts already on the fruit, no added lab yeast needed. The mouth gets sealed, often with beeswax, and the vessel sits buried so the earth holds a stable temperature around it, cool and even (UNESCO, 2013). The wine then ages inside for months to several years. The ground is doing the work that a modern winery does with steel tanks and climate control.
Why does this matter so much? Three reasons. First, age: this is about 8,000 years of continuous use, which makes it the oldest winemaking method still practiced today (Phillips, 2000). Second, recognition: in 2013, UNESCO listed Georgia's qvevri winemaking as Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity (UNESCO, 2013). Third, what it represents: it is an indigenous knowledge system, discovered and refined by trial across thousands of years, with no chemistry textbook behind it. And the style it produces, the amber or orange wine made by leaving white grapes on their skins, is now celebrated by winemakers worldwide.
Think back to Season 1. We saw Kichwa guayusa and Fijian kava ceremonies where the ritual itself carried the knowledge forward, generation to generation. The qvevri is the same idea in clay. The vessel is the teacher. Nobody had to write the method down for it to survive, because the practice itself remembers.

[PICTURE] Look at the panel on this lesson. It shows a map of wine's ancient origins in Georgia and the UNESCO heritage listing. Now picture the pot itself: a tall clay egg, buried to its neck in a courtyard, only the sealed mouth showing above the soil. Underground, in the dark and the cool, grapes are quietly turning into wine the exact way they did before the pyramids were built.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the Georgian qvevri is a clay vessel buried in the earth, used for about 8,000 years of continuous winemaking, recognized by UNESCO in 2013 as Intangible Cultural Heritage, and it is a living indigenous knowledge system where the vessel itself, not a textbook, carries the method (UNESCO, 2013; Phillips, 2000).

[CHECK] Quick check. Why is the qvevri called a living example of indigenous knowledge? [Beat] Because the method was discovered and refined over about 8,000 years of continuous practice and survives through the tradition itself, the buried clay vessel, rather than through written instructions, and UNESCO recognized it in 2013 (UNESCO, 2013).

## Key Takeaways
- A qvevri is a large, egg-shaped clay vessel (about 100 to 1,000 liters) buried in the earth up to its neck (UNESCO, 2013).
- Crushed grapes ferment from wild yeasts, the vessel is sealed and buried so the earth holds a stable temperature, and the wine ages for months to years (UNESCO, 2013).
- It is about 8,000 years old, the oldest winemaking method still in use, and the source of amber or orange wine (Phillips, 2000).
- UNESCO recognized Georgia's qvevri tradition as Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity in 2013; like Kichwa guayusa and Fijian kava, the vessel carries the knowledge (UNESCO, 2013).

## Sources
- UNESCO. (2013). Ancient Georgian traditional Qvevri wine-making method. Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity.
- Phillips, R. (2000). A short history of wine. HarperCollins.

module_title: Episode 9: Wine — Blood of the Earth
title: The Judgment of Paris
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can analyze how a 1976 blind tasting disrupted the prestige of French wine.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we studied the Georgian qvevri. Two quick questions.
One: what is a qvevri? [Beat] A large, egg-shaped clay vessel buried in the earth, used to ferment and age wine for about 8,000 years (UNESCO, 2013).
Two: what did UNESCO recognize in 2013? [Beat] Georgia's qvevri winemaking tradition as Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity (UNESCO, 2013).

[HOOK] For centuries, the rule was simple: France made the best wine, and everyone else made the rest. Then, on one afternoon in Paris in 1976, a small group of French experts sat down to taste some wine without seeing the labels. What they picked broke the rule, and it broke something else too, the idea that the label tells you the truth.

[TEACH] The event was a blind tasting organized in Paris in 1976 by a British wine merchant named Steven Spurrier (Taber, 2005). The judges were all French wine experts, the people whose whole job was knowing French wine was the best. Spurrier set up a contest comparing California wines against France's finest. Blind means the judges could not see the bottles. No labels, no regions, no prestige. Just the wine in the glass.
The result stunned everyone. A 1973 Chateau Montelena Chardonnay from Napa Valley beat the top French white Burgundies. And a 1973 Stag's Leap Cabernet Sauvignon, also from Napa, beat the most prestigious red Bordeaux (Taber, 2005). California won both the whites and the reds, judged by French palates.
The aftermath says as much as the result. The French establishment tried to suppress the outcome; the loss was an embarrassment they did not want spread. But the only journalist in the room worked for Time magazine, and the story went around the world (Taber, 2005). California's prestige and prices soared almost overnight. The winning Stag's Leap bottle eventually entered the Smithsonian. And in 2006, a rematch was held with the same wines aged thirty years, and California won again (Taber, 2005).
Here is the lesson worth keeping. The blind tasting removed one thing: the label. And the moment the label was gone, the prestige bias went with it. The experts could no longer give French wine extra credit for being French, because they did not know which was which. That tells you something uncomfortable. The story we attach to a wine, the famous region, the old name, shapes how we taste it, sometimes more than the wine itself does. Prestige is not just a price tag. It is a lens that changes perception.

[PICTURE] Look at the panel on this lesson, the reference to the Judgment of Paris. Now picture the room: a line of identical glasses, no bottles in sight, French judges leaning in, swirling and sniffing, writing down scores. They think they are scoring France. They are actually scoring Napa, and they are about to find out that without the label, they could not tell the difference.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: in 1976, French expert judges in a Paris blind tasting picked California wines, a 1973 Chateau Montelena and a 1973 Stag's Leap, over France's best, and because the labels were hidden the prestige bias disappeared, which shows that the story we tell about a wine shapes how we perceive it (Taber, 2005).

[CHECK] Quick check. Why did the blind tasting produce a different result than a labeled one? [Beat] Because hiding the labels removed the prestige bias, so the judges could no longer give French wine extra credit for its name, which suggests prestige shapes perception (Taber, 2005).

## Key Takeaways
- The Judgment of Paris was a 1976 blind tasting organized by British merchant Steven Spurrier, judged by French experts, comparing California wines to France's finest (Taber, 2005).
- A 1973 Chateau Montelena Chardonnay and a 1973 Stag's Leap Cabernet Sauvignon from Napa beat the top French whites and reds (Taber, 2005).
- The French establishment tried to suppress it, but Time magazine spread the story, California's prices soared, the Stag's Leap bottle entered the Smithsonian, and a 2006 rematch saw California win again (Taber, 2005).
- Removing the label removed the prestige bias, which suggests prestige shapes how we perceive quality.

## Sources
- Taber, G. M. (2005). Judgment of Paris: California vs. France and the historic 1976 Paris tasting. Scribner.

Useful for Social Studies and Economics.","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Judgment_of_Paris_(wine)","source_url":"Reference summary with primary-source links"}]

module_title: Episode 9: Wine — Blood of the Earth
title: The Dop System
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can analyze how paying workers in alcohol functioned as exploitation and social control, and understand its lasting harm.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 8 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we examined the Judgment of Paris. Two quick questions.
One: what did the blind tasting remove? [Beat] The label, and with it the prestige bias, so judges could not give French wine extra credit for its name (Taber, 2005).
Two: what happened to California prestige after 1976? [Beat] It soared; prices rose, the Stag's Leap bottle entered the Smithsonian, and California won the 2006 rematch (Taber, 2005).

[HOOK] This is the hard part of the wine story, and it deserves an honest telling. In South Africa, for generations, some farmworkers were not paid only in money. They were paid, in part, in wine. Cheap wine, handed out during the workday, counted as wages. It was called the dop system, and it was not a kindness. It was a way to control people.

[TEACH] First, a note on framing. This is honest history, not an endorsement of drinking. Alcohol caused real and lasting harm here, and the point is to understand how a product was turned against the very people who made it.
The word dop is Afrikaans for a small tot, a little cup of alcohol. The dop system was the practice of paying South African wine farmworkers, predominantly Black and Coloured South Africans, partly in cheap wine instead of full wages (London, 1999).
The history runs deep. It began in the colonial Cape Colony of the 17th and 18th centuries. It continued under apartheid, the system of racial segregation in force from 1948 to 1994. The practice was formally outlawed in 1961, but it kept going informally for decades, and it was finally pushed out by post-apartheid labor reforms starting in 1994 (London, 1999). So even after a law said stop, it did not stop for a long time.
Now, how did it work as social control? This is the cruel logic. By handing workers alcohol through the day, the farms created and fed alcohol dependence. A worker who depends on the daily dop is tied to the farm that supplies it. The drink becomes a leash. It kept wages low and kept workers bound to the place (London, 1999). The product the workers harvested was used to trap them.
And the harm did not end when the practice did. The legacy is measurable. The Western Cape, the heart of South African wine country, has some of the highest rates of Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorders in the world, a direct result of generations of the dop system feeding alcohol into farming communities (May et al., 2007). Children are born today carrying the damage of a labor practice that targeted their grandparents. That is how long this reaches.
Connect this back to Season 1. We saw sugar built on enslaved labor and coffee built on plantation systems. Wine belongs in that same hard truth. The drink we have called sacred and prestigious was also, here, built on the exploitation of the people who produced it.

[PICTURE] Look at the panel on this lesson. Picture a vineyard in the Western Cape at midday. Rows of vines, hands moving fast, and a cart coming down the row, not with water, but with cheap wine poured into tin cups. The pay for the morning's work is the very thing slowly taking the worker's health, and their children's. That image is the dop system in one frame.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the dop system paid South African wine farmworkers, mostly Black and Coloured South Africans, partly in cheap wine instead of wages, which created alcohol dependence as a tool of social control to keep them tied to the farm, and its documented legacy is some of the world's highest rates of Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorders in the Western Cape (London, 1999; May et al., 2007).

[CHECK] Quick check. How did the dop system work as both exploitation and social control? [Beat] By paying workers in cheap wine instead of full wages, it created alcohol dependence that kept them bound to the farm and unable to leave or demand better, and its harm continues in the Western Cape's high rates of Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorders (London, 1999; May et al., 2007).

## Key Takeaways
- The dop system (dop is Afrikaans for a small tot) paid South African wine farmworkers, predominantly Black and Coloured South Africans, partly in cheap wine instead of full wages (London, 1999).
- It began in the colonial Cape Colony (17th and 18th centuries), continued under apartheid (1948 to 1994), was outlawed in 1961 but continued informally, and ended with post-apartheid reforms from 1994 (London, 1999).
- It worked as social control by creating alcohol dependence that tied workers to the farm (London, 1999).
- Its lasting harm includes some of the world's highest rates of Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorders in the Western Cape; like sugar and coffee, wine too was built on the exploitation of those who produced it (May et al., 2007).

## Sources
- London, L. (1999). The dop system, alcohol abuse and social control amongst farm workers in South Africa. Social Science & Medicine, 48(10), 1407-1414.
- May, P. A., et al. (2007). The epidemiology of fetal alcohol syndrome and partial FAS in a South African community. Drug and Alcohol Dependence, 88(2-3), 259-271.

module_title: Episode 9: Wine — Blood of the Earth
title: The Business of Prestige
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how a geographic name on a label creates economic value far beyond the cost of making the wine.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we looked at the dop system in South Africa. Two quick questions.
One: what did the dop system mean? [Beat] Paying farmworkers partly in cheap wine instead of full wages, which created alcohol dependence and trapped them on the farms (London, 1999).
Two: name one documented legacy of the dop system. [Beat] The Western Cape carries some of the world's highest rates of fetal alcohol spectrum disorder, a direct result of generations of the practice (May et al., 2007).

[HOOK] Here is a number that sounds impossible. Two bottles of wine, grown in the same kind of soil, made the same way, sitting on opposite sides of an invisible line in the dirt. One sells for thirty dollars. The other sells for three thousand. The grapes did not do that. A name did that. Let me show you how.

[TEACH] Start with the size of the thing. The global wine market brings in about three hundred forty billion dollars a year as of twenty twenty four (Statista, 2024). But the inside of that market is split in a strange way. Industrial bulk wine, the everyday bottles, makes up roughly sixty five percent of all the wine by volume, yet it earns only about thirty five percent of the revenue, because those bottles sell for five to fifteen dollars each. At the very top, ultra premium wine is only about two percent of the volume, but it commands two hundred to five thousand dollars or more a bottle (Storchmann, 2012). So a tiny sliver of the wine takes home a giant share of the money. That gap is the whole story of wine economics.

What creates that gap? Appellation. An appellation is a legally protected geographic brand, a name tied to a place and defended by law. Think of the Bordeaux AOC in France. Inside that boundary, a First Growth estate can sell a bottle for five hundred to five thousand dollars. Now walk across the boundary to an adjacent vineyard, similar soil, similar winemaking, the same skill in the cellar. That neighbor's wine sells for fifteen to forty dollars (Storchmann, 2012). The grapes are nearly identical. The difference in price is almost entirely narrative and certification, the story the name carries and the law that protects it.

Here is why this matters beyond wine. A certification like an appellation does real work. It sets standards, it creates exclusivity so only producers inside the zone can use the name, and it builds centuries of reputation that a newcomer cannot copy quickly. That protects tradition, which sounds purely good. But the same system acts as a barrier to entry. It keeps the economic premium locked inside the old European regions and makes it hard for anyone outside to break in. And we know quality is not actually trapped inside those borders, because the Judgment of Paris in nineteen seventy six showed French judges, tasting blind, preferring California wines to France's finest. The prestige outlived the proof. Even after the world saw that great wine can come from anywhere, the names from the old places kept commanding the higher prices (Storchmann, 2012).

[PICTURE] Picture two vineyards touching, separated by a single thin line drawn on a map. The vines lean over that line toward each other. Same sun, same soil, same hands pruning them. On one side, the bottle wears a famous name and sells for thousands. On the other side, no name, and the bottle sells for the price of a sandwich. The line in the dirt is invisible. The line on the label is worth a fortune.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the global wine market is about three hundred forty billion dollars a year (Statista, 2024), and within it a geographic name, an appellation, can multiply a bottle's price a hundred times over a near identical neighbor, because the value lives in the story and the certification, not just the liquid (Storchmann, 2012). A name on a label is a brand the law protects.

[CHECK] Quick check. Two adjacent vineyards have similar soil and similar winemaking, but one bottle sells for thousands and the other for tens of dollars. What explains almost all of that difference? [Beat] The appellation, the legally protected geographic name and the reputation it carries, not the grapes or the craft (Storchmann, 2012).

## Key Takeaways
- The global wine market is worth about three hundred forty billion dollars a year as of twenty twenty four (Statista, 2024).
- Bulk wine is most of the volume but a smaller share of revenue; ultra premium wine is a tiny share of volume but commands huge prices (Storchmann, 2012).
- An appellation is a legally protected geographic name; inside the Bordeaux boundary a top wine sells for hundreds to thousands, while a similar neighbor outside sells for tens of dollars.
- These systems protect tradition but also act as barriers to entry that preserve European market power, even after the Judgment of Paris showed quality is not geographically confined.

## Sources
- Statista. (2024). Global wine market revenue 2024. Statista Research Department.
- Storchmann, K. (2012). Wine economics. Journal of Wine Economics, 7(1), 1-33.

module_title: Episode 9: Wine — Blood of the Earth
title: Wine as an Asset, and the Boycott That Worked
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how fine wine became an investment, and how a grape boycott linked the choices of shoppers to the lives of farm workers.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 8 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we looked at the business of prestige. Two quick questions.
One: how does an appellation create value? [Beat] It is a legally protected geographic name, so the story and the certification, not just the grapes, push a bottle's price far above a similar neighbor's (Storchmann, 2012).
Two: how big is the global wine market? [Beat] About three hundred forty billion dollars a year as of twenty twenty four (Statista, 2024).

[HOOK] Two things can be true about the same bottle of wine. It can sit in a temperature controlled warehouse for years as a financial investment, gaining value like a stock. And the grapes inside it can have been picked by hands working in conditions so harsh that millions of shoppers refused to buy them. Today, both stories. The wine as money, and the grapes as a fight for dignity.

[TEACH] First, wine as a financial asset. Fine wine behaves a bit like rare art. The supply is fixed, because a great vineyard only makes so many bottles in a great year, and once people drink them, the older vintages get rarer. That scarcity, plus reputation, means fine wine prices have historically risen around ten to fifteen percent a year, though it swings a lot by vintage and producer (Storchmann, 2012). Wine even invented its own futures market, called en primeur. Bordeaux estates sell their wine about two years before it is bottled, so a buyer commits to a price long before the wine is in hand. There is even an index, the way there is for stocks. The Liv-ex Fine Wine 100 tracks the hundred most traded wines in the world, and from two thousand four to twenty twenty two it grew about three hundred fifty percent (Storchmann, 2012). But this is not free money. Wine is illiquid, meaning you cannot always sell quickly when you need cash. There are counterfeits, fakes dressed up as famous bottles. And there are storage costs, because the wine must be kept in climate controlled conditions or its value evaporates.

Now the second story, which connects that polished world of indexes and futures to the people in the fields. From nineteen sixty five to nineteen seventy, the United Farm Workers ran a grape boycott in California. The union was founded by Cesar Chavez and Dolores Huerta, and it organized agricultural workers, most of them Mexican American, who picked grapes under harsh conditions with no right to bargain collectively (Martin, 2011). Chavez and Huerta did something clever. They connected the choice in the grocery store directly to the conditions in the field, and asked the whole country to stop buying California table grapes.

It worked. Roughly fourteen to seventeen million Americans joined the boycott (Martin, 2011). None of those shoppers worked on a grape farm. None of them had a contract with a grower. And yet by simply not buying, they squeezed the growers hard enough to force the table. In nineteen seventy, major grape growers signed contracts with the United Farm Workers that finally brought wages, health benefits, and labor protections to the pickers. That is the economic lesson to carry. A consumer boycott can pressure producers even when the consumers have no direct economic relationship with them at all.

[PICTURE] Hold two images side by side. In one, a quiet warehouse, racks of dark bottles resting in cool air, each one a number on an index ticking upward. In the other, a hot California field, hands moving fast under the sun. Now picture the thread that ties them, a shopper in a grocery aisle who pauses, looks at a bunch of grapes, and puts it back down. That small refusal, repeated millions of times, reached all the way back to the field.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: fine wine became an investment asset, with scarcity, en primeur futures, and a Liv-ex index that rose about three hundred fifty percent from two thousand four to twenty twenty two (Storchmann, 2012), while the grape boycott of nineteen sixty five to nineteen seventy, with fourteen to seventeen million Americans taking part, won contracts for the pickers and proved consumers can pressure producers they never deal with directly (Martin, 2011).

[CHECK] Quick check. Why was the United Farm Workers grape boycott an effective economic tool, and what idea does it illustrate? [Beat] Millions of shoppers refused to buy grapes and squeezed growers into signing contracts; it shows that consumer boycotts can pressure producers even when the consumers are not in a direct economic relationship with them (Martin, 2011).

## Key Takeaways
- Fine wine became a financial asset through scarcity, appreciation around ten to fifteen percent a year, and en primeur futures where Bordeaux is sold about two years before bottling (Storchmann, 2012).
- The Liv-ex Fine Wine 100 index grew about three hundred fifty percent from two thousand four to twenty twenty two, but wine carries real risks: illiquidity, counterfeits, and storage costs (Storchmann, 2012).
- The United Farm Workers grape boycott of nineteen sixty five to nineteen seventy, founded by Cesar Chavez and Dolores Huerta, drew fourteen to seventeen million participants and won nineteen seventy contracts with wages, health benefits, and labor protections (Martin, 2011).
- A consumer boycott can pressure producers even when the consumers have no direct economic relationship with them.

## Sources
- Storchmann, K. (2012). Wine economics. Journal of Wine Economics, 7(1), 1-33.
- Martin, P. L. (2011). Importing poverty? Immigration and the changing face of rural America. Yale University Press.

module_title: Episode 9: Wine — Blood of the Earth
title: Wine in Persian Poetry
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can analyze how classical Persian poets used wine as a layered metaphor.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we treated wine as an asset and looked at the boycott that worked. Two quick questions.
One: name one risk of wine investment. [Beat] A bottle can spoil, fakes are common, and prices can fall, so your money is tied up in something fragile.
Two: what did the United Farm Workers boycott connect? [Beat] It connected the wine in the glass to the labor of the people who picked the grapes.

[HOOK] Imagine reading a poem about getting drunk in a tavern and realizing, slowly, that the poet may not be talking about wine at all. For hundreds of years, the greatest poets of Persia wrote about wine while meaning two things at once. Learning to read both is the whole skill.

[TEACH] One of the great literary traditions involving wine comes from classical Persian poetry, and in it wine works as a complex, layered metaphor (Lewisohn, 2010). Sometimes it is literally a drink. Often it stands for divine love, spiritual transcendence, or the wisdom of living fully in the present moment.

Three major voices anchor this tradition. Omar Khayyam, who lived from 1048 to 1131, wrote the verses gathered as the Rubaiyat, where wine stands for present-moment joy and where mortality becomes an argument for taking that pleasure now (Lewisohn, 2010). Rumi, who lived from 1207 to 1273, used intoxication as an image of spiritual union, the self dissolving into the divine the way a drinker loses ordinary control. Hafez, who lived from 1315 to 1390, made wine a symbol of divine beauty and of spiritual liberation set against rigid religious orthodoxy (Lewisohn, 2010). Same image, three different shades of meaning.

Now the key that unlocks all of it. In the Sufi Islamic tradition, a text carries two levels at once: zahir, the outer meaning, and batin, the inner meaning (Lewisohn, 2010). Persian wine poetry lives in both at the same time. The wine is real wine AND a symbol for divine grace. So a reader who takes only the literal meaning misses the spiritual dimension entirely. And a reader who throws out the literal meaning, who insists it is "only" a symbol, misses the earthly pleasure the poet also wanted. The poem refuses to choose, and you are not supposed to choose either. You hold both.

That is what makes this poetry hard and rewarding. The skill is not picking the "right" reading. The skill is holding the literal and the symbolic together and asking what the two of them, stacked, create.

[PICTURE] Picture a single cup of red wine on a low table in fourteenth-century Shiraz. Look at it one way and it is just a drink, sweet and warming. Look again and the same cup is the love of God, overflowing. The poet, Hafez, is grinning, because he made one cup mean both, and he is daring you to set either meaning down.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: classical Persian poets like Khayyam, Rumi, and Hafez used wine as a layered metaphor, literal drink and divine love at once, and the Sufi idea of zahir and batin, outer and inner meaning, tells you to read both layers together rather than choosing one (Lewisohn, 2010). Two meanings, one cup.

[CHECK] Quick check. What do zahir and batin mean, and why do they matter for reading Persian wine poetry? [Beat] Zahir is the outer meaning and batin is the inner meaning; they matter because the wine is real wine AND a symbol for divine grace, so you have to read both layers at once instead of choosing (Lewisohn, 2010).

## Key Takeaways
- Classical Persian poetry uses wine as a complex, layered metaphor, sometimes a literal drink, often divine love, transcendence, or present-moment wisdom (Lewisohn, 2010).
- Omar Khayyam (1048 to 1131) used wine for present-moment joy and mortality; Rumi (1207 to 1273) for spiritual union; Hafez (1315 to 1390) for divine beauty and liberation (Lewisohn, 2010).
- Zahir and batin are the outer and inner meanings of a text in Sufi tradition.
- The wine is real wine AND a symbol of divine grace, so skilled reading holds both meanings together.

## Sources
- Lewisohn, L. (2010). Hafez and wine: Metaphor and ambiguity. In The Oxford handbook of Persian poetry (pp. 341-365). Oxford University Press.

module_title: Episode 9: Wine — Blood of the Earth
title: The Symposium
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can analyze why Plato set serious philosophy at a wine-drinking party.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we read wine in Persian poetry. Two quick questions.
One: what do zahir and batin mean? [Beat] Zahir is the outer meaning of a text and batin is the inner meaning, and Persian wine poetry uses both at once (Lewisohn, 2010).
Two: what did wine symbolize for Rumi or Hafez? [Beat] For Rumi, intoxication meant spiritual union with the divine; for Hafez, wine meant divine beauty and spiritual liberation (Lewisohn, 2010).

[HOOK] Here is a strange choice. One of the most important books in the history of philosophy, a book about love, beauty, and the soul, is set at a party where everyone is drinking. Plato could have set it in a classroom. He chose a wine party instead. Why?

[TEACH] The book is Plato's Symposium, written around 385 to 370 BCE (Plato, c. 385 BCE). It is a philosophical dialogue set at a drinking party in Athens, where the guests agree to take turns giving speeches in praise of love. Wine flows the whole time. Out of that party comes some of Greek philosophy's deepest thinking about love and beauty.

To read it well, you need to know what a symposium actually was. It was a real social institution of ancient Athens, not just a casual get-together (Phillips, 2000). Male citizens reclined on couches arranged in a circle. A symposiarch, a kind of drinking master, decided the wine-to-water ratio for the evening, since the Greeks mixed their wine with water and the ratio set how wild the night would get. Music, poetry, philosophical talk, and political debate were all normal symposium activities. So the party was already a place where Athenians mixed pleasure and serious thought.

Now the literary question. Why would Plato choose this setting, a drinking party, instead of a lecture hall or a law court, for his most serious ideas? The choice itself is an argument. It suggests that in Athenian culture wine and wisdom were not enemies but companions, that loosening the tongue with wine could loosen the mind toward truth. The setting tells you something the speeches alone do not.

And watch one detail. Plato shows Socrates drinking more than anyone at the party, yet never appearing drunk (Plato, c. 385 BCE). That is not a throwaway line. It hints at Socrates' philosophical self-mastery: he can take in the wine, the pleasure, the full human experience, and stay clear-headed and in command of himself. The wine that overcomes the others cannot overcome him. The setting becomes a quiet test, and Socrates passes it.

[PICTURE] Picture a circle of couches in lamplight, cups being refilled, voices rising as the night goes on. One by one the men give speeches about love, some slurring a little as the wine works on them. In the middle is Socrates, drinking cup after cup, perfectly steady, thinking as clearly at the end as at the start. The party is the point, and his steadiness inside it is the point too.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: Plato set the Symposium at an Athenian drinking party on purpose, because in that culture wine and wisdom went together, and he made Socrates drink the most without getting drunk to show his self-mastery (Plato, c. 385 BCE; Phillips, 2000). The setting is an argument.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why did Plato set serious philosophy at a drinking party, and what does Socrates' drinking suggest? [Beat] The setting suggests Athenian culture linked wine with wisdom rather than treating them as opposites; Socrates drinking the most without getting drunk suggests his philosophical self-mastery (Plato, c. 385 BCE; Phillips, 2000).

## Key Takeaways
- Plato's Symposium (about 385 to 370 BCE) is a dialogue set at an Athenian drinking party where guests give speeches praising love (Plato, c. 385 BCE).
- The symposium was a real Athenian institution: men reclined in a circle, a symposiarch set the wine-to-water ratio, and music, poetry, and debate followed (Phillips, 2000).
- Choosing a drinking party over a lecture hall suggests Athenian culture linked wine and wisdom.
- Socrates is shown drinking more than anyone without getting drunk, hinting at his philosophical self-mastery (Plato, c. 385 BCE).

## Sources
- Plato. (c. 385 BCE). Symposium.
- Phillips, R. (2000). A short history of wine. HarperCollins.

module_title: Episode 9: Wine — Blood of the Earth
title: The Language of Wine
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can analyze wine criticism as a literary genre that builds experience with words.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we sat in on Plato's Symposium. Two quick questions.
One: why did Plato set philosophy at a drinking party? [Beat] To suggest that Athenian culture linked wine with wisdom, so the setting itself is part of the argument (Phillips, 2000).
Two: what did Socrates' drinking suggest? [Beat] That he had philosophical self-mastery, since he drank more than anyone yet never got drunk (Plato, c. 385 BCE).

[HOOK] How do you describe a taste to someone who is not tasting it? You cannot hand them the flavor. You can only hand them words. Wine writers have built an entire genre out of that problem, and the words they pick do something sneaky: they almost create the experience for you.

[TEACH] Wine criticism is a genre, a category of writing with its own conventions and its own highly specialized vocabulary, and that vocabulary works to construct subjective experience in the reader's mind (Colman, 2008). Look at where the words come from. Critics reach for fruit, calling a wine black cherry or cassis. They reach for the earth, naming leather, tobacco, or truffle. They reach for structure with touch-words, firm tannins, silky, round. They reach for time, praising a long finish that lingers after you swallow. And they reach for place, calling a wine mineral, flinty, or chalky. Notice that almost none of this is literal. There is no cherry in the bottle. These are metaphors, comparisons that let a reader feel a taste they cannot taste.

That language also carries power, and one critic shows how much. In the 1980s, the American critic Robert Parker introduced a 100-point rating system for wine (Colman, 2008). A score above 90 turned into a marketing tool overnight. The economics were striking: wines that earned 95 points or higher often saw their retail prices rise 20 to 50 percent within weeks of the score being published (Colman, 2008). Two numbers from one writer could move the price of a bottle that much. That is language and authority doing real work in the world.

So here is the critical-reading skill, and it is the heart of the lesson. When you read a wine review, do three things. First, notice which metaphors the writer uses, fruit, earth, structure, time, or place, because the choice reveals how the writer wants you to feel. Second, watch how the writer builds a sense of place, the terroir, through words alone. Third, and this is the one most readers skip, notice what the writer leaves out. What is absent? A review that never mentions the people who farmed the grapes, or the price, or the marketing behind the score, is telling you something by its silence. Reading well means hearing the silences too.

[PICTURE] Picture two reviews of the same red wine side by side. One is thick with words, black cherry, supple tannins, a long mineral finish, and by the end you can almost taste a glass you have never held. The other just says "tastes good, costs a lot." The first one built an experience out of metaphor. Now ask of the rich one: what did all those beautiful words quietly leave out?

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: wine criticism is a literary genre that uses sensory metaphors, fruit, earth, structure, time, and place, to construct experience in words, and a score from a critic like Robert Parker can raise a wine's price 20 to 50 percent, so read for the metaphors used, the place built, and the things left unsaid (Colman, 2008). Words make the taste.

[CHECK] Quick check. When you read a wine review as a literary genre, what three things should you analyze? [Beat] The sensory metaphors the writer uses, how the writer builds a sense of place through language, and what the writer leaves out or does not describe (Colman, 2008).

## Key Takeaways
- Wine criticism is a literary genre with a specialized vocabulary that constructs subjective experience in words (Colman, 2008).
- Its metaphors draw from fruit, earth, structure, time, and place, almost none of them literal.
- Robert Parker's 100-point system, introduced in the 1980s, made a score above 90 a marketing tool, with 95-plus wines often rising 20 to 50 percent in price within weeks (Colman, 2008).
- Critical reading means noticing the metaphors used, the sense of place built, and what the writer leaves out.

## Sources
- Colman, T. (2008). Wine politics. University of California Press.

module_title: Episode 9: Wine — Blood of the Earth
title: Key Terms: Wine
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can define the core vocabulary from Episode 9 across all four lenses.
Subject: Vocabulary | Run time: about 5 minutes

Your word bank for the Wine episode. Read it quick, out loud if you can. The full glossary lives in the course Resources.

## Geography

**Vitis vinifera** (VEE-tis vih-NIF-er-ah). The common wine grape, which grows best between about 30 and 50 degrees latitude (Johnson & Robinson, 2019).

**Terroir** (tehr-WAHR). The taste of place, the full environment of soil, climate, slope, water, and craft that shapes a wine (Wilson, 1998).

**Viticulture** (VIH-tih-kul-cher). The science and practice of growing grapes.

**Phylloxera** (fih-LOK-ser-ah). A root-feeding insect from North America that destroyed most of Europe's vineyards in the late 1800s (Campbell, 2004).

**Rootstock.** The root system, usually from resistant American vines, onto which wine grapes are grafted.

## Social Studies

**Qvevri** (KWEV-ree). A large egg-shaped clay vessel buried in the earth, used in Georgia to ferment and age wine for about 8,000 years (UNESCO, 2013).

**Symposium** (sim-POH-zee-um). A formal Greek drinking party where philosophy, poetry, and politics happened over wine.

**Eucharist** (YOO-kah-rist). The Christian sacrament in which wine is made sacred, adopted by the 4th century CE (Phillips, 2000).

**Dop system.** The practice of paying South African farmworkers partly in cheap wine, a tool of exploitation and social control (London, 1999).

**AOC.** The French appellation system, formalized in 1935, that turned geographic identity into protected intellectual property.

## Economics

**Appellation** (ap-uh-LAY-shun). A legally defined and protected wine region, which acts as a geographic brand (Storchmann, 2012).

**Prestige** (press-TEEZH). Social standing from reputation, the main force driving high wine prices.

**En primeur** (on pree-MUHR). Selling wine as futures before it is bottled, with buyers committing about two years early.

**Vintage.** The year the grapes were harvested, which affects quality and investment value.

## English Language Arts

**Metaphor.** Comparing two unlike things, as wine stands for divine love in Persian poetry (Lewisohn, 2010).

**Zahir and Batin** (zah-HEER, bah-TEEN). The outer and inner meaning of a text in Sufi tradition, both present at once (Lewisohn, 2010).

**Blind tasting.** Judging wine without seeing the label, which removes prestige bias (Taber, 2005).

**Genre** (ZHAHN-ruh). A category of writing with its own conventions, like wine criticism (Colman, 2008).

## Sources
- Campbell, C. (2004). The botanist and the vintner: How wine was saved for the world. Algonquin Books.
- Colman, T. (2008). Wine politics. University of California Press.

module_title: Episode 9: Wine — Blood of the Earth
title: Cumulative Review: Wine
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can recall the top facts from all four lenses and see how they connect.
Subject: Review | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Let's pull the Wine episode together, one quick fact per lens, then the thread that ties them.

[TEACH] Start with geography. Wine grapes grow in two bands at about 30 to 50 degrees latitude, and they make their best wine when stressed by poor, well-drained soil (Johnson & Robinson, 2019; Wilson, 1998). The key idea is terroir, the taste of place, where even a band of fossil limestone under Burgundy shows up in the glass. And we saw how fragile that geography is: phylloxera, an insect from North America, killed about 70 percent of Europe's vineyards before the cure, grafting onto American rootstock, saved it (Campbell, 2004).

Now social studies. Wine is older than writing, found in Georgia around 6000 BCE, and it became both sacred, in the Greek symposium and the Christian Eucharist, and a marker of power (McGovern et al., 2017; Phillips, 2000). The Georgian qvevri, a buried clay vessel, has made wine for 8,000 years and is UNESCO heritage (UNESCO, 2013). The 1976 Judgment of Paris showed that prestige, not just quality, drives reputation (Taber, 2005). And the dop system reminds us, honestly, that wine too was built on exploited labor, with lasting harm (London, 1999).

Then economics. The global wine market is about 340 billion dollars, and a protected name like the Bordeaux AOC can make a wine sell for a hundred times more than a near-identical bottle next door (Statista, 2024; Storchmann, 2012). Fine wine became an investment asset, with the Liv-ex 100 up about 350 percent from 2004 to 2022, while the UFW grape boycott showed that consumer choice can change labor conditions (Storchmann, 2012; Martin, 2011).

Last, language. Persian poets used wine as a layered metaphor for divine love, holding outer and inner meaning at once (Lewisohn, 2010). Plato set his philosophy at a wine-soaked symposium, linking wine to wisdom. And wine criticism became a genre, where a Robert Parker score could move a price by half (Colman, 2008).

[REMEMBER] Here is the thread. Wine is the drink of place. Terroir ties the soil to the glass, history ties the glass to the sacred and to power, economics ties a name to a price, and language ties the taste to metaphor. And, as the season keeps reminding us, the beauty sits alongside real harm.

[CHECK] One last check before the quiz. What single idea ties the whole wine episode together, and what does it mean? [Beat] Terroir, the taste of place: wine carries the soil, slope, and weather of one specific vineyard, so geography ends up in the glass (Wilson, 1998).

## Key Takeaways
- Geography: wine grapes grow at about 30 to 50 degrees, stressed soil makes complexity, and terroir puts place in the glass (Wilson, 1998).
- Social studies: wine is ancient and sacred, preserved in the Georgian qvevri, disrupted at the Judgment of Paris, and shadowed by the dop system (UNESCO, 2013; Taber, 2005; London, 1999).
- Economics: a protected name creates enormous value, fine wine became an asset, and the UFW boycott linked consumers to labor (Storchmann, 2012).
- Language: from Persian poetry to Plato to Parker scores, wine lives in metaphor and persuasion (Lewisohn, 2010; Colman, 2008).

## Sources
- Campbell, C. (2004). The botanist and the vintner. Algonquin Books.
- Colman, T. (2008). Wine politics. University of California Press.
- Johnson, H., & Robinson, J. (2019). The world atlas of wine (8th ed.). Mitchell Beazley.
- Lewisohn, L. (2010). Hafez and wine: Metaphor and ambiguity. In The Oxford handbook of Persian poetry (pp. 341-365). Oxford University Press.
- London, L. (1999). The dop system, alcohol abuse and social control amongst farm workers in South Africa. Social Science & Medicine, 48(10), 1407-1414.
- Martin, P. L. (2011). Importing poverty? Immigration and the changing face of rural America. Yale University Press.
- McGovern, P. E., et al. (2017). Early Neolithic wine of Georgia in the South Caucasus. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 114(48), E10309-E10318.
- Phillips, R. (2000). A short history of wine. HarperCollins.
- Statista. (2024). Global wine market revenue 2024. Statista Research Department.
- Storchmann, K. (2012). Wine economics. Journal of Wine Economics, 7(1), 1-33.
- Taber, G. M. (2005). Judgment of Paris. Scribner.
- UNESCO. (2013). Ancient Georgian traditional Qvevri wine-making method. Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity.
- Wilson, J. E. (1998). Terroir. University of California Press.

module_title: Episode 9: Wine — Blood of the Earth
title: Knowledge Check: Wine
text_content:
This check pulls from all four lenses in Episode 9: geography, social studies, economics, and language.

Each time you take it, you get 10 questions drawn from a larger pool, and the answer choices are shuffled, so a retake is never the same test twice. You need 80 percent to pass, and you can retake it as many times as you like. The goal is understanding, not gatekeeping.

After each question you see an explanation and a citation. Read them. The reasons a wrong answer is wrong are the best study material here.

When you finish, take the Project assignment for this episode, where the bigger questions, how terroir puts place in a glass and whether prestige or quality sets a wine's price, get the long-form treatment they deserve.

only"}],"correctOptionId":"q4a","explanation":"Burgundy's fossil-rich Kimmeridgian limestone gives its Pinot Noir a distinct mineral quality, and the same rock layer can be traced and tasted from Chablis to England.","citation":"Lesson: Terroir, the Taste of Place"},{"id":"q5","questionText":"What was phylloxera, and what did it Crisis"},{"id":"q6","questionText":"How was the phylloxera crisis finally solved?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q6a","text":"By grafting European vines onto pest-resistant solved"}],"correctOptionId":"q6a","explanation":"Growers grafted European fruiting vines onto resistant American roots, which is why more than 99 percent of the world's wine grapes now grow on American rootstock.","citation":"Lesson: The Phylloxera Crisis"},{"id":"q7","questionText":"Where and roughly when was the oldest documented wine made?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q7a","text":"The country of Georgia, around 6000 BCE"},{"id":"q7b","text":"France, around 1500 CE"},{"id":"q7c","text":"California, in 1976"},{"id":"q7d","text":"Egypt, around 100 CE"}],"correctOptionId":"q7a","explanation":"Wine residue in clay vessels in the country of Georgia dates to about 6000 BCE, the oldest documented wine on Earth (McGovern et al., 2017).","citation":"Lesson: Wine, Power, and the Sacred"},{"id":"q8","questionText":"How did Christianity shape wine's place in Western region"},{"id":"q9c","text":"A type of grape"},{"id":"q9d","text":"A wine tax"}],"correctOptionId":"q9a","explanation":"A qvevri is a large, egg-shaped clay vessel, buried up to its neck, used in Georgia for wild-yeast fermentation, storage, and aging.","citation":"Lesson: The Georgian Qvevri"},{"id":"q10","questionText":"Why is the Georgian qvevri tradition significant?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q10a","text":"It is about 8,000 years old, the oldest winemaking method still in use, named UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage in 2013"},{"id":"q10b","text":"It was invented in the 1900s"},{"id":"q10c","text":"It is used only for grape juice"},{"id":"q10d","text":"It is banned today"}],"correctOptionId":"q10a","explanation":"The qvevri method has about 8,000 years of continuous use, the oldest still in use, and UNESCO recognized it as Intangible Cultural Heritage in 2013.","citation":"Lesson: The Georgian Qvevri"},{"id":"q11","questionText":"What happened at the Judgment of Paris in 1976?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q11a","text":"In a blind tasting judged by French experts, sacred"}],"correctOptionId":"q11a","explanation":"Steven Spurrier's 1976 blind tasting, judged by French experts, saw a Napa Chardonnay and a Napa Cabernet beat the top French whites and reds.","citation":"Lesson: The Judgment of Paris"},{"id":"q12","questionText":"What did the blind format of the Judgment of Paris reveal about removed the prestige bias, so the judges scored on taste alone, showing how much prestige normally shapes perception.","citation":"Lesson: The Judgment of Paris"},{"id":"q13","questionText":"What was control"},{"id":"q13b","text":"A wine-rating system"},{"id":"q13c","text":"A grape variety"},{"id":"q13d","text":"A French appellation law"}],"correctOptionId":"q13a","explanation":"The dop system paid mostly Black and Coloured South African farmworkers partly in cheap wine, creating alcohol dependence as a means of labor control.","citation":"Lesson: The Dop System"},{"id":"q14","questionText":"What is a documented legacy of the dop system?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q14a","text":"The Western Cape has some of the world's highest rates of Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorders"},{"id":"q14b","text":"Higher wine quality"},{"id":"q14c","text":"No measurable effect"},{"id":"q14d","text":"Lower grape yields"}],"correctOptionId":"q14a","explanation":"Generations of the dop system left the Western Cape with some of the world's highest rates of Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorders, a direct documented harm.","citation":"Lesson: The Dop System"},{"id":"q15","questionText":"Roughly how large is the global wine market, and how is it billion dollars; all premium"},{"id":"q15c","text":"There is no global market"},{"id":"q15d","text":"Premium wine is most of the volume"}],"correctOptionId":"q15a","explanation":"The global wine market is about 340 billion dollars a year, with industrial bulk wine at roughly 65 percent of volume but only about 35 percent of revenue.","citation":"Lesson: The Business of geographic name lets a First Growth sell for far more than a near-identical wine outside the boundary"},{"id":"q16b","text":"It lowers the price of all wine"},{"id":"q16c","text":"It changes the soil chemistry"},{"id":"q16d","text":"It has no effect on price"}],"correctOptionId":"q16a","explanation":"A Bordeaux First Growth can sell for 500 to 5,000 dollars while an adjacent vineyard with similar soil sells for 15 to 40, so the gap is almost entirely narrative and certification.","citation":"Lesson: The Business of Prestige"},{"id":"q17","questionText":"What is the economic paradox of AOC systems?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q17a","text":"They protect tradition but also act as barriers to entry that preserve established market power"},{"id":"q17b","text":"They make all wine free"},{"id":"q17c","text":"They guarantee equal prices"},{"id":"q17d","text":"They have no downside"}],"correctOptionId":"q17a","explanation":"Appellation systems protect quality and tradition, but they also build barriers that keep new producers competing against centuries of inherited prestige.","citation":"Lesson: The Business of Prestige"},{"id":"q18","questionText":"How has
