risks"},{"id":"q18b","text":"It always loses value"},{"id":"q18c","text":"It cannot be bought or sold"},{"id":"q18d","text":"It has no risks"}],"correctOptionId":"q18a","explanation":"Fine wine has appreciated historically, with the Liv-ex 100 up about 350 percent from 2004 to 2022, but it carries illiquidity, counterfeit, and storage risks.","citation":"Lesson: Wine as an Asset, and the Boycott That Worked"},{"id":"q19","questionText":"What did the United Farm Workers grape boycott (1965 to 1970) demonstrate?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q19a","text":"Consumer boycotts can pressure producers even when consumers have no direct economic tie to them"},{"id":"q19b","text":"Boycotts never work"},{"id":"q19c","text":"Only governments can change labor conditions"},{"id":"q19d","text":"Grapes cannot be boycotted"}],"correctOptionId":"q19a","explanation":"Led by Cesar Chavez and Dolores Huerta, the boycott drew 14 to 17 million Americans and won 1970 labor contracts, showing consumer choice can pressure producers.","citation":"Lesson: Wine as an Asset, and the Boycott That Worked"},{"id":"q20","questionText":"How does wine function in classical Persian poetry?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q20a","text":"As a layered metaphor, sometimes literal drinking, often divine love or spiritual transcendence"},{"id":"q20b","text":"Only as a literal drink"},{"id":"q20c","text":"Only as a poison"},{"id":"q20d","text":"It never appears in Persian poetry"}],"correctOptionId":"q20a","explanation":"Poets like Khayyam, Rumi, and Hafez used wine as a complex metaphor for present-moment joy, divine love, and spiritual liberation, as well as literal wine.","citation":"Lesson: Wine in Persian Poetry"},{"id":"q21","questionText":"What do the Sufi terms zahir and batin mean?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q21a","text":"The outer and inner meaning of a text, both present at once"},{"id":"q21b","text":"Two grape varieties"},{"id":"q21c","text":"Wine and water"},{"id":"q21d","text":"Two Persian poets"}],"correctOptionId":"q21a","explanation":"Zahir is the outer meaning and batin the inner, so Persian wine poetry is both real wine and a symbol for divine grace at the same time.","citation":"Lesson: Wine in Persian Poetry"},{"id":"q22","questionText":"Why did Plato set his Symposium at a drinking party?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q22a","text":"It suggests a link between wine and wisdom in Athenian culture, with the party as a setting for serious philosophy"},{"id":"q22b","text":"Because lecture halls did not exist"},{"id":"q22c","text":"To discourage philosophy"},{"id":"q22d","text":"By accident"}],"correctOptionId":"q22a","explanation":"By choosing a symposium rather than a lecture hall, Plato tied wine to wisdom, with the drinking party as a real Athenian institution for music, poetry, and debate.","citation":"Lesson: The Symposium"},{"id":"q23","questionText":"What does Socrates drinking heavily without getting drunk suggest in the Symposium?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q23a","text":"His philosophical self-mastery"},{"id":"q23b","text":"That he disliked wine"},{"id":"q23c","text":"That wine was watered down"},{"id":"q23d","text":"Nothing at all"}],"correctOptionId":"q23a","explanation":"Socrates is shown out-drinking everyone while staying clear-headed, a detail that points to his self-mastery and philosophical control.","citation":"Lesson: The Symposium"},{"id":"q24","questionText":"What is the Robert Parker effect in wine criticism?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q24a","text":"His 100-point rating system turned a high score into a marketing tool that raised prices"},{"id":"q24b","text":"He banned wine ratings"},{"id":"q24c","text":"He invented terroir"},{"id":"q24d","text":"He lowered all wine prices"}],"correctOptionId":"q24a","explanation":"Parker's 100-point system, introduced in the 1980s, made a score above 90 a marketing tool, with 95-plus wines often rising 20 to 50 percent in price within weeks.","citation":"Lesson: The Language of Wine"},{"id":"q25","questionText":"Why is wine criticism described as a literary genre?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q25a","text":"It uses a specialized vocabulary of sensory metaphors to construct subjective experience in words"},{"id":"q25b","text":"It is written only in poetry"},{"id":"q25c","text":"It avoids all description"},{"id":"q25d","text":"It is purely numerical"}],"correctOptionId":"q25a","explanation":"Wine writing draws metaphors from fruit, earth, structure, time, and place to build a sense of experience, which makes it a genre worth reading critically.","citation":"Lesson: The Language of Wine"},{"id":"q26","questionText":"Which region was once the leading U.S. wine producer before phylloxera and Prohibition?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q26a","text":"The Ohio River Valley, including Indiana"},{"id":"q26b","text":"Napa Valley"},{"id":"q26c","text":"Bordeaux"},{"id":"q26d","text":"Marlborough"}],"correctOptionId":"q26a","explanation":"In the mid-1800s the Ohio River Valley, including Indiana, was the leading U.S. wine region, before phylloxera and Prohibition wiped out its vineyards.","citation":"Lesson: Terroir, the Taste of Place"},{"id":"q27","questionText":"What role did the Greek symposium play in Athenian life?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q27a","text":"A formal social institution where philosophy, poetry, and politics happened over wine"},{"id":"q27b","text":"A religious fast"},{"id":"q27c","text":"A type of vineyard"},{"id":"q27d","text":"A wine tax office"}],"correctOptionId":"q27a","explanation":"The symposium was a formal Athenian institution where male citizens reclined and a symposiarch set the wine-to-water ratio for an evening of music, poetry, and debate.","citation":"Lesson: The Symposium"},{"id":"q28","questionText":"Why is wine described as the drink of place more than beer is?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q28a","text":"Wine carries the soil, slope, and weather of one specific vineyard, while beer is more a portable recipe"},{"id":"q28b","text":"Wine has no connection to geography"},{"id":"q28c","text":"Beer is older than wine"},{"id":"q28d","text":"Wine is made anywhere identically"}],"correctOptionId":"q28a","explanation":"Through terroir, wine expresses the exact place it grew, while beer can be brewed to a recipe almost anywhere, so wine is the drink of place.","citation":"Lesson: Blood of the Earth (intro)"},{"id":"q29","questionText":"What does it mean that over 99 percent of wine grapes grow on American rootstock?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q29a","text":"Even famous European wines stand on a hidden American root system because of the phylloxera cure"},{"id":"q29b","text":"All wine is made in America"},{"id":"q29c","text":"American grapes taste better"},{"id":"q29d","text":"European vines were never replanted"}],"correctOptionId":"q29a","explanation":"The phylloxera cure grafted European vines onto resistant American roots, so nearly every famous French, Italian, and Spanish wine grows on an American foundation.","citation":"Lesson: The Phylloxera Crisis"},{"id":"q30","questionText":"What is en primeur in the wine trade?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q30a","text":"Selling wine as futures, before bottling, with investors committing about two years early"},{"id":"q30b","text":"A blind tasting"},{"id":"q30c","text":"A grape disease"},{"id":"q30d","text":"A French appellation"}],"correctOptionId":"q30a","explanation":"En primeur is the Bordeaux practice of selling wine as futures before it is bottled, with buyers committing to prices roughly two years in advance.","citation":"Lesson: Wine as an Asset, and the Boycott That Worked"},{"id":"q31","questionText":"How does climate shape the acid and sugar balance in wine?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q31a","text":"Cool sites give fresh, high-acid, lower-alcohol wine; warm sites give riper, fuller wine"},{"id":"q31b","text":"Climate has no effect on wine"},{"id":"q31c","text":"Warm sites always make sour wine"},{"id":"q31d","text":"Cool sites make the strongest wine"}],"correctOptionId":"q31a","explanation":"As part of terroir, cool climates preserve acidity for fresh, lower-alcohol wine, while warm climates ripen grapes for fuller, riper wine.","citation":"Lesson: Terroir, the Taste of Place"},{"id":"q32","questionText":"What recurring pattern does the dop system share with Season 1 commodities?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q32a","text":"A commodity built on the exploitation of those who produce it, like sugar's enslaved labor and coffee's plantations"},{"id":"q32b","text":"A focus on premium pricing"},{"id":"q32c","text":"A reliance on volcanic soil"},{"id":"q32d","text":"No connection to labor"}],"correctOptionId":"q32a","explanation":"Like sugar's enslaved labor and coffee's plantation systems, the dop system shows a commodity economy resting on the exploitation of its producers.","citation":"Lesson: The Dop System"},{"id":"q33","questionText":"What is an appellation?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q33a","text":"A legally defined wine-producing
